query: The photo shows active water seepage at a basement wall joint. What previous case is most relevant and what should we do next?
query_image_caption: a construction site photo of a concrete wall

hits.title: Basement wall water ingress observed at construction joint
hits.recommended_action: Mark and photograph the full extent of seepage and note elevation/chainage references.; Inspect the joint detailing, membrane continuity, and any penetrations or tie-hole repairs.; Check adjacent drainage, sump operation, and external water management conditions.; Implement temporary water control measures and isolate any affected electrical or finish-sensitive areas.; Raise a corrective action for waterproofing specialist review and retesting after repair.
hits.escalation: Escalate immediately if leakage increases, reaches active electrical equipment, or shows signs of wall movement, cracking, or soil washout.

hits.title: Hairline crack observed near lift core wall opening
hits.recommended_action: Measure and mark crack length, width, and orientation using a calibrated gauge.; Check whether the crack is live by monitoring for seasonal or load-related movement.; Review recent pours, stripping sequence, and temporary loading around the core wall.; Inspect for associated moisture ingress, spalling, or adjacent cracking patterns.; Escalate for structural review if the crack widens, propagates, or appears through-thickness.
hits.escalation: Escalate to structural engineer if crack width exceeds project tolerance, shows displacement, or is accompanied by leakage or progressive propagation.

hits.title: Concrete column honeycombing detected after form removal
hits.recommended_action: Sound the affected area and carry out a depth assessment by engineer or competent inspector.; Verify reinforcement cover and determine whether structural repair is required.; Review pour sequence, vibration records, and formwork tightness for the affected lift.; Protect the area from impact and restrict loading until disposition is confirmed.; Prepare a repair method statement if patching, epoxy injection, or breakout and reinstatement is needed.
hits.escalation: Escalate to structural engineer immediately if defect depth approaches reinforcement, cover is compromised, or multiple columns exhibit similar honeycombing.

answer:
The photo you provided shows active water seepage at a basement wall joint. Based on the retrieved evidence, the most relevant incident ID for this situation is INC-001. Here are the next steps you should take:

1. Mark and photograph the full extent of the seepage and note the elevation and chainage references. This information will help in understanding the scale and location of the problem.

2. Inspect the joint detailing, the continuity of the waterproofing membrane, and check for any penetrations or ties and sealant repairs. This step is crucial to identify the root cause of the water ingress.

3. Check the adjacent drainage and sump operations to ensure they are functioning correctly. External water management conditions should also be reviewed to rule out any contributing factors from the surroundings.

4. Implement temporary water control measures to prevent further damage. This might include isolating any affected electrical or finish-sensitive areas.

5. Prepare a corrective action for a waterproofing specialist to review and retest after the repair work is completed.

If the leakage increases, reaches active electrical equipment, or shows signs of wall movement, cracking, or soil washout, you should escalate the situation immediately to the appropriate level of structural or waterproofing expertise.

Grounded in the evidence from incident ID INC-001, these actions are specifically tailored to address the high-severity water ingress issue you've observed at the basement wall joint.
